title: Droves of “Sephora kids” are buying anti-aging products despite the health risks posed by these products. Companies are pocketing profits by selling cosmetic products —
prompt_preview:
[1 chunks] Task: Extract all distinct claims about AI or emerging technology in this press release. Do NOT assume a fixed number.
Output JSON only matching the provided schema.
Return concise, standalone claim_texts that could be tested against scientific literature. Exclude purely procedural statem

  "claims": [
    "Children as young as eight who use anti-aging products could experience irreversible skin problems.",
    "Common ingredients in anti-aging skin products, such as retinols, glycolic acid, and ascorbic acid, are used to improve the appearance of fine lines and wrinkles by
raw_text:
```json
{
  "claims": [
    "Children as young as eight who use anti-aging products could experience irreversible skin problems.",
    "Common ingredients in anti-aging skin products, such as retinols, glycolic acid, and ascorbic acid, are used to improve the appearance of fine lines and wrinkles by boosting collagen production and increasing cell turnover.",
    "Retinols can cause rashes and increase the risk of sunburn and sun damage.",
    "Skincare products meant for adults can lead to skin irritation, redness, breakouts, and long-term skin damage in children.",
    "Cell renewal rates change as people age, with babies and young children having a faster cell turnover rate than adults.",
    "Children have more sensitive skin than adults due to a thinner epidermis and a weaker skin barrier.",
    "Regulatory bodies like the U.S. Food and Drug Administration do not require testing of cosmetic products such as anti-aging creams on children.",
    "Families with teens aged between 13 and 17 increased their skincare purchases by 28.6% over 2023.",
    "Families with tweens aged 6 to 12 grew their skincare purchases by 27.2%."
  ]
}
```
